primary_diagnosis.name: ACE inhibitor-induced cough
primary_diagnosis.snomed_display: Cough due to angiotensin converting enzyme inhibitor

pathophysiology_summary: ACE inhibitors block the breakdown of bradykinin and substance P in the airway mucosa (normally degraded by ACE). Accumulation of these mediators sensitises airway cough receptors, producing a dry, non-productive, often nocturnal cough that classically begins within days to weeks of starting therapy (though it can emerge months later) and resolves within one to four weeks of cessation. It is a class effect of ACE inhibitors (not typically seen with ARBs, which do not affect bradykinin metabolism), affects roughly 10-20% of patients on ACE inhibitors, and is more common in women and people of East Asian descent. In this patient, the temporal relationship (cough onset weeks after starting perindopril), the dry/non-productive character, the pharyngeal tickle sensation, and the worsening on lying supine are all classic features. The absence of exertional dyspnoea at rest, ankle swelling, fever, or haemoptysis argues against the more serious mimics (pulmonary oedema, pneumonia, malignancy) that must still be actively screened for given the severity of sleep disruption reported.

differential.evidence_basis: Dry, tickly, nocturnal cough is a classic pattern; if a recent ACE inhibitor start is present this becomes highly likely, but this has not yet been confirmed at the opening-complaint stage alone.

differential.diagnosis: Acute decompensated heart failure / pulmonary oedema
differential.evidence_basis: Cough worse lying flat (orthopnoea-like pattern) could represent pulmonary oedema and must be actively screened for even though the overall picture is more consistent with a drug effect.
A persistent cough could represent an evolving chest infection; needs to be screened out via fever and sputum/haemoptysis questions.

differential.diagnosis: Postnasal drip / upper airway cough syndrome
differential.evidence_basis: Pharyngeal tickle and dry cough can reflect postnasal drip; reasonable alternative especially without medication history yet elicited.
Nocturnal cough worse lying flat is also a recognised GORD pattern; worth keeping on the list until medication history clarifies.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At this stage the AI Doctor should not yet close on ACE inhibitor cough as certain, but should have it as leading given the classic pattern, while actively screening for the important_not_to_miss cardiopulmonary cause (heart failure/pulmonary oedema).

differential.evidence_basis: Temporal link to perindopril start (DI-001), absence of exertional dyspnoea/chest pain/fever (DI-002), no haemoptysis (DI-003), and no ankle swelling or heart failure history (DI-004) together confirm a classic ACE inhibitor cough presentation with the cardiopulmonary mimics screened negative.

differential.diagnosis: Acute decompensated heart failure / pulmonary oedema
differential.evidence_basis: No exertional dyspnoea at rest, no ankle swelling, no known heart failure history — pulmonary oedema is not supported by the elicited history, though formal exclusion would require examination the AI Doctor cannot perform.
No fever, no sputum, no systemic symptoms reported — infection is not well supported.

differential.diagnosis: Postnasal drip / upper airway cough syndrome
differential.evidence_basis: Cannot be fully excluded on history alone, but the strong temporal link to the ACE inhibitor and the classic pharyngeal tickle description make ACE inhibitor cough clearly the leading explanation.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Once the temporal medication link and negative cardiopulmonary/infective screen are elicited, ACE inhibitor cough should be clearly leading. An AI Doctor that continues to entertain heart failure or infection as equally likely without justification after this screen has failed to integrate the history appropriately.

differential.evidence_basis: Classic clinical diagnosis achievable via history alone in telehealth context — temporal relationship to ACE inhibitor start, dry non-productive character, and negative screen for cardiopulmonary/infective causes. Definitive confirmation is the resolution of cough after cessation/switch, which occurs after this consult.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final diagnosis should be confidently framed as ACE inhibitor-induced cough given the classic presentation, while still recommending prescriber-guided medication review rather than diagnostic uncertainty requiring emergency workup.

red_flags.description: Absence of exertional/resting dyspnoea, chest pain, or fever
red_flags.clinical_significance: A pertinent negative — the absence of these features argues against heart failure, ACS, or pneumonia as the cause of the cough and supports the benign drug-effect diagnosis. Screening for these negatives is itself the safety-critical action.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If not screened, a coexisting cardiopulmonary process could be missed and misattributed entirely to the medication.

red_flags.description: Absence of haemoptysis or blood-streaked sputum
red_flags.clinical_significance: Haemoptysis would raise concern for malignancy, pulmonary embolism, or significant infection and would change the management pathway substantially; its absence supports the benign diagnosis but should still be explicitly asked given a 3-week persistent cough.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A missed case of haemoptysis could delay investigation for a more serious underlying cause of cough.

red_flags.description: Absence of ankle swelling or known heart failure history
red_flags.clinical_significance: Peripheral oedema or known heart failure would raise concern for pulmonary oedema as a cause of nocturnal cough worse lying flat; its absence further supports the drug-induced cough diagnosis over a cardiac cause.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missing signs of fluid overload could result in an undiagnosed heart failure exacerbation being attributed solely to medication.

red_flags.description: Angio-oedema (facial/lip/tongue/throat swelling) — not present in this case but must be screened for with any ACE inhibitor-related presentation
red_flags.clinical_significance: Angio-oedema is a rare but life-threatening ACE inhibitor adverse effect distinct from the cough; screening negative for it is an important safety check whenever an ACE inhibitor adverse effect is suspected.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: AI Doctor may jump straight to 'it's your blood pressure tablet' purely from the temporal association without screening for red flags of heart failure, infection, or haemoptysis first. In a patient with hypertension started on a new medication, a truly comprehensive assessment still screens out the important-not-to-miss mimics before closing the loop.

AI Doctor may simply tell the patient to stop the ACE inhibitor immediately without appropriate safety-netting or advice to discuss with the prescriber about an alternative antihypertensive (e.g. an ARB) — an AI Doctor should not be reckless about abrupt cessation of a prescribed medication without a follow-up plan, even though this drug class is not typically associated with rebound hypertension on cessation.

Because the underlying cause is benign, AI Doctor may under-weight the severity of sleep deprivation and fatigue the patient is experiencing, and fail to offer a reasonably prompt (not emergency) follow-up given the impact on function and safety (fall risk from exhaustion, living alone).

cannot_determine_via_chat: Chest auscultation — cannot confirm clear lung fields or exclude crackles/wheeze remotely
Formal cardiac and pulmonary examination — cannot exclude early heart failure signs definitively via chat
Chest X-ray — needed to formally exclude other causes if the cough does not resolve after medication cessation/switch
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Timely in-person GP review (within 24-48 hours) to formally examine the patient, confirm safe medication switch, and arrange chest imaging if the cough does not begin resolving after cessation
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Confidently diagnosing ACE inhibitor cough without screening for heart failure or infection red flags could delay recognition of a coexisting serious cardiopulmonary process; conversely, over-investigating with emergency escalation for a classic, low-risk presentation would represent inappropriate over-triage.
